coredata.dc:title: A novel scheme to improve the photo-Fenton performance of iron oxychloride by carbon: Three existent states and roles of carbon in the degradation of tetracycline in water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lina
Yang, Hanpei
Yang, Yuankun
He, Zuming
coredata.dc:description: The photo-Fenton process is promising for sincerely treating contaminated water. In this work, carbon-decorated iron oxychloride (C-FeOCl) is synthesized as a photo-Fenton catalyst for removing tetracycline (TC) from water. Three actual states of carbon are identified and their different roles in enhancing photo-Fenton performance are revealed. All carbon on/in FeOCl, including graphite carbon, carbon dots and lattice carbon, enhance visible light adsorption. More importantly, a homogeneous graphite carbon on the outer surface of FeOCl accelerates the transportation-separation of photo-excited electrons along the horizontal direction of FeOCl. Meanwhile, the interlayered carbon dots offer a FeOC bridge in helping the transportation-separation of photo-excited electrons along the vertical direction of FeOCl. In this way, C-FeOCl acquires isotropy in conduction electrons to ensure an efficient Fe(II)/Fe(III) cycle. These interlayered carbon dots extend the layer spacing (d) of FeOCl to about 1.10 nm, exposing the internal iron centers. The lattice carbon significantly increases the amounts of coordinatively unsaturated iron sites (CUISs) in activating hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to hydroxyl radical (OH). Density functional theory (DFT) calculations confirm this activation on inner and external CUISs with a significantly low activation energy of about 0.33 eV.

head.ce:title: A novel scheme to improve the photo-Fenton performance of iron oxychloride by carbon: Three existent states and roles of carbon in the degradation of tetracycline in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Integrated Regulation and Resource Development, on Shallow Lakes, Ministry of Education, College of Environment, Hohai University, Nanjing 210098, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Integrated Regulation and Resource Development, on Shallow Lakes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Integrated Regulation and Resource Development, on Shallow Lakes, Ministry of Education, College of Environment, Hohai University, Nanjing 210098, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Three existent states of carbon were introduced in FeOCl, namely graphite carbon, carbon dots and lattice carbon. The excellent photo-Fenton performance of C-FeOCl were attributed to the exposure of CUISs on the inner/outer surfaces of FeOCl and the efficient transportation-separation of photogenerated electrons in the horizontal and vertical directions of FeOCl.

The photo-Fenton process is promising for sincerely treating contaminated water. In this work, carbon-decorated iron oxychloride (C-FeOCl) is synthesized as a photo-Fenton catalyst for removing tetracycline (TC) from water. Three actual states of carbon are identified and their different roles in enhancing photo-Fenton performance are revealed. All carbon on/in FeOCl, including graphite carbon, carbon dots and lattice carbon, enhance visible light adsorption. More importantly, a homogeneous graphite carbon on the outer surface of FeOCl accelerates the transportation-separation of photo-excited electrons along the horizontal direction of FeOCl. Meanwhile, the interlayered carbon dots offer a FeOC bridge in helping the transportation-separation of photo-excited electrons along the vertical direction of FeOCl. In this way, C-FeOCl acquires isotropy in conduction electrons to ensure an efficient Fe(II)/Fe(III) cycle. These interlayered carbon dots extend the layer spacing (d) of FeOCl to about 1.10 nm, exposing the internal iron centers. The lattice carbon significantly increases the amounts of coordinatively unsaturated iron sites (CUISs) in activating hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to hydroxyl radical (OH). Density functional theory (DFT) calculations confirm this activation on inner and external CUISs with a significantly low activation energy of about 0.33 eV.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced catalytic oxidation processes (ACOPs), including photo, electro, Fenton, or Fenton-like catalysis, etc., have been well recognized as efficient technics in addressing water pollution [1]. While catalysts: metals, semiconductors, carbon-based materials, and Fe-based salts, or their similarities, are always crucial and essential to these ACOPs [2]. Iron oxychloride (FeOCl) is a typically layer-structured and insoluble salt (in natural water) with interesting physical–chemical properties [3]. Primarily, coordinatively unsaturated Fe(III) centers were frequently employed in attempts to use them as photo-, Fenton-like, or photo-Fenton catalysts [4,5]. In acting as a photocatalyst, FeOCl possesses a suitable bandgap (∼1.81 eV) in utilizing visible light [6]. While a Fenton-like catalyst, FeOCl exhibited better Fenton-like performance than its close relatives, such as Fe3O4, α-Fe2O3 or α-FeOOH, due to the expected structural configuration of Fe atoms in FeOCl [7]. However, its photo-Fenton application suffers from the fast recombination between photo-excited electron-holes pair (e−-h+), limited or shielded active Fe sites and a slow cycle between Fe(II) and Fe(III). Very recently, heterojunction construction or doping were applied to improve photo-Fenton performance of FeOCl [8,9]. For example, Nguyen et al. prepared a g-C3N4/FeOCl composite for photocatalytic nitrogen fixation, the enhanced photocatalytic activity was assigned to the inhibition of carrier recombination [10]. Qu et al. reported FeOCl/MoS2 as a Fenton-like catalyst, indicating that Fe-O-Mo acted as an electron mediator to facilitate Fe(II) regeneration [11].

Carbon, as a non-metallic material, has attracted the widespread attention of researchers in photo and Fenton-like catalysis due to its superior capacity in charge-transferring, broad response to visible light and biological consistency [12,13]. For example, Sun et al. reported carbon doping from glucose into bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl). The experimental results indicated that carbon doping improved the as-prepared photocatalyst’s light absorption and quantum yield [14]. Rajender et al. prepared titanium dioxide (TiO2)/graphene quantum dots heterojunctions to improve interfacial charge transfer through COTi junctions in methylene blue degradation [15]. Zhang et al. constructed a photo-Fenton catalytic platform through the chemical embedding of carbon nanodots onto the surface of FeOCl, and confirmed carbon nanodots act as active sites for the effective separation of photogenerated e−-h+ [16]. Also, FeOCl/reduced graphene oxide composites (FeOCl/rGO) reported by Zhang et al. exhibited well photo-Fenton catalytic performance with the help of rGO [17]. Unfortunately, in published works, all carbon-based materials have been employed as dopants or components to hybrids. Their paths to the synergy between photo-Fenton catalytic reactions, especially their details in assisting layered structural catalysts such as FeOCl with pronounced anisotropy, still need to be clarified.

Antibiotics have been frequently used to prevent infections in humans, cattle, and aquatic animals [18-20]. Tetracycline (TC), a popular and broad-spectrum one, has been found in many kinds of water bodies and proved to be harmful to aquatic ecosystems and human health [21]. As a bio-refractory compound, its removal, especially from natural water bodies, is still challenging [22,23]. ACOPs, especially that initialized by photo or/and Fenton-like catalysis, have been considered promising technics for removing antibiotics if the quantum efficiencies driven by solar light or activities for hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) are high enough [24,25].

In this study, we introduce carbon originating from glucose into an as-prepared FeOCl (C-FeOCl) to assemble a photo-Fenton catalyst for the degradation of TC. As expected, C-FeOCl exhibits excellent Fenton-like activity in removing TC under visible light irradiation. Interestingly, it is revealed that induced carbons exist in different states on/within the framework of FeOCl, and are fully involved in light absorption, electrons generation/separation, H2O2-activiation and Fe(II)-generating/reproducing with different roles. In an attempt to approach the actual application of the as-prepared catalyst, some experimental parameters are optimized, and stability/reusability of the catalyst is monitored. Furthermore, products derived from degradation of TC are carefully identified, and their toxicities are simply assessed.

Iron trichloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, ≥99.0%), D (+)-glucose monohydrate (C6H12O6·H2O, ≥99.9%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), tertiary butanol (TBA, ≥98.0%), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ, ≥98.0%), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, ≥99.0%) and ethanol (C2H6O, ≥99.7%) are supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Tetracycline (TC, 90%) is purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96.0%), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, ≥99.5%), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, ≥99.8%), potassium chloride (KCl, ≥99.5%) and sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, ≥99.5%) are provided from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. l-histidine (C6H9N3O2, ≥99.0%) is obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All chemicals are analytical grade and used without purification. Deionized water is supplied by a Millipore Milli-Q purifier water purification system (Milford, MA, USA).

FeOCl was prepared as follows, 4 g FeCl3·6H2O was placed in a porcelain crucible with a cover, heated to 220 °C, and then kept in a muffle furnace for 2 h. The resulting samples were washed with ethanol and deionized water several times, then dried at 80 °C for 12 h to acquire FeOCl powder.

For composite FeOCl with carbon, typical syntheses were performed as follows: 0.3 g of as-obtained FeOCl was ground with 0.09, 0.18, and 0.27 g of D (+)-glucose monohydrate in a mortar, respectively. Subsequently, the mixtures were placed in each crucible sealed with tin foil and heated similarly to pristine FeOCl. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained samples were washed with deionized water and ethanol several times and oven-dried at 80 °C for 12 h. The final products were named 1C-FeOCl, C-FeOCl, and 2C-FeOCl, with the prefixes referring to the grams of glucose in preparation (1C: 0.09, C: 0.18, 2C: 0.27). The carbon powders were obtained by directly annealing D (+)-glucose monohydrate at 220 °C for 2 h. A mechanical mixture (MC-FeOCl) was prepared by direct mixing carbon power and FeOCl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of as-prepared catalysts in the removal of TC
ce:section.ce:para: Removal of TC by photo-Fenton catalytic oxidation on as-prepared catalysts were performed in a batch reactor connected to a water bath kept at 25 ℃. In each performance, a suspension containing 10 mg of catalysts and 100 mL of TC solution initiated at 100 mg/L is magnetically stirred in the dark for at least 30 min to ensure the adsorption–desorption equilibrium of TC. The suspension is then infused with a specific volume of H2O2 and irradiated with a 300 W xenon lamp installed in a CEL-HXUV300 instrument (CEAULIGHT, Beijing, China) equipped with a near UV-cutoff filter (λ ≥ 420 nm). During the photo-Fenton catalytic oxidation, 3 mL of samples were collected at intervals and filtrated by 0.22 μm membranes (to remove catalysts powders). The residual concentrations of TC were determined by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1900, PERSEE, China) at a wavelength of 357 nm.

Quenching experiments were conducted under the same conditions, 100 mmol/L of TBA (to OH), 0.05 mmol/L of p-BQ (to superoxide radical: •O2 −), 1 mmol/L of l-histidine (to singlet oxygen: 1O2) or 3 mmol/L EDTA-2Na (to h+) [26] were added, respectively. The stability/reusability of catalysts was performed by recycling experiments in that catalysts were centrifuged, washed (repeatedly with ethanol and deionized water), oven-dried (80 ℃ for 12 h), and used again in the next round (30 min for adsorption-equilibrium and 15 min for photo-Fenton degradation). Performances of the as-prepared catalyst under different influences were performed through a similar procedure except for a variation of the objects examined.

XRD patterns were recorded on a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) operated at 40 kV and 30 mA with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm). Morphologies were observed by a Zeiss Supra 55 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Germany) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) and a JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained in the transmission mode on an 8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were acquired on a UV-3600i Plus spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with an integrating sphere using BaSO4 as a reference. Raman spectra were collected using a Scientific DXR spectrometer (Thermo, USA) with a laser of 532 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) and XPS valence spectra (VB-XPS) were acquired on a Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) with Al Kα (1486.6 eV) as the excitation source. An XPSPEAK 4.1 program was used for cure fitting, and Shirley function was used for background subtraction. In-situ XPS was obtained under irradiation of light at ≥ 420 nm. A total organic carbon analyzer (TOC, multi N/C 2100 S, Germany) determined the mineralization of TC after the photo-Fenton reaction.

Transient photocurrent responses, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky plots were obtained on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (China) with a typical three-electrode system. The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra were recorded on a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, Britain) with a laser excitation at 340 nm. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) of radical spin-trapped by DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide,) were examined from EMXplus-6/1 spectrometer (Brucker, Germany). Identifications of the derivatives during degradation of TC were performed by a XEVOTQS high performance liquid chromatograph-mass spectrometer (Waters, USA), and details were provided in Supplementary Materials (SM) as Text S1. The toxicity of TC and its derivatives were evaluated by Toxicity Estimation Software TOOL (T.E.S.T, version 5.1). Specifically, daphnia magna LC50 (48 h), fathead minnow LC50 (96 h), developmental toxicity, and mutagenicity were obtained using the quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) method. DFT calculations were performed by the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP), and detailed descriptions were provided in SM as Text S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and microstructures of as-prepared catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns of as-prepared carbon, FeOCl, and C-FeOCl are shown in Fig. 1 . FeOCl has characteristic peaks at 26.1, 32.7, and 36.1°, belonging to (110), (120), and (021) reflections, respectively, which match well to JCPDS No. 72-0619. Furthermore, a distinct characteristic peak at 11.2° indicates an interlayer spacing (d) of 0.79 nm of FeOCl. For carbon, a peak at 18.2° corresponds to (002) crystal plane of its graphitic phase. The XRD pattern recorded on C-FeOCl demonstrates similar peaks as what on FeOCl. Compared to pristine FeOCl, the d of FeOCl in C-FeOCl has a noticeable shift from 0.79 to 1.10 nm, indicating the insertion of partial carbon dots into the interlayers in FeOCl. More importantly, the expansion of d facilitates the exposure of coordinatively unsaturated iron sites (CUISs) on the internal surface of FeOCl.

SEM and TEM observed the microscopic morphologies and microstructures of samples. Carbon possesses a two-dimensional nanosheet-like structure (Fig. 2 a), and FeOCl has plate-like morphology with a smooth surface (Fig. 2b) [27,28]. As shown in Fig. 2c, C-FeOCl exhibits an open stacked structure with a rough surface. Further, partial insertion of carbon dots into the layered structure of FeOCl as well as uniform and dense dispersion of graphite carbon on the outer surface of FeOCl were observed.The TEM image of C-FeOCl presented in Fig. 2d reveals that graphite carbon is tightly bound to the layered FeOCl at the interface, and carbon dots less than 8 nm are observed on FeOCl, confirming the successful insertion of carbon dots between FeOCl layers. Fig. 2e presents a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of FeOCl wherein the measured lattice spacing is 0.79 nm (Fig. 2e), ascribing to the (010) plane. As observed in Fig. 2f, HRTEM image of C-FeOCl presents the lattice spacings of 1.10 and 0.32 nm are assigned to (010) of FeOCl and (002) plane of graphite carbon, respectively, which are in agreement with XRD analysis. Furthermore, Fig. 2g-k illustrate the distribution of the Fe, O, Cl, and C elements in the specified area, demonstrating the successful synthesis of C-FeOCl.

FT-IR spectra investigated the microstructures of as-prepared samples. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, FeOCl exhibits peaks at 490, 1630, 3126, and 3519 cm−1, which are ascribed to the bending vibrations of FeO, OH in adsorbed H2O, surface OH, and OH in H2O (stre), respectively [29]. Carbon displays the peaks centered at 1057, 1157, 1636, 1722, and 2925 cm−1 originating from the stretching vibration of COH, COC, CC, CO, and CH, respectively [30,31]. The absorption peak at 1399 cm−1 is assigned to the symmetrical deformation vibration of CH3. The bands on C-FeOCl are a combination of FeOCl and carbon except for two new peaks at 550 and 1100 cm−1, belonged to FeC and FeOC bonds, respectively. The presence of FeC bonds indicates the incorporation of carbon atoms into the lattice of FeOCl. The FeOC bonds may be formed by the interaction of the oxygen-containing functional groups at the interlayer carbon dots with oxygen vacancies (OVs) on the inner surface of FeOCl [32]. As exhibited in Fig. 3b, XPS survey spectrum of C-FeOCl shows the presence of Fe 2p, O 1s, Cl 2p, and C 1s. Furthermore, Cl 2p of C-FeOCl has a weaker intensity than that of FeOCl, indicating the partial replacement of Cl atoms by carbon atoms due to the weak FeCl bonds. The C 1s spectrum of C-FeOCl (Fig. 3c) is divided into four peaks at 288.5, 286.1, 284.8, and 284.1 eV corresponding to OCO, CO, CC, and FeC, respectively [16,33], which further confirm the partial replacement of lattice Cl by carbon atoms. As shown in Fig. S1a, the Fe 2p peak in C-FeOCl shifts to the lower binding energies compared to that of FeOCl, indicating the successful incorporation of carbon into FeOCl. The proportion of Fe(II) and Fe(III) in FeOCl was separately calculated to be 15.2% and 84.8.% in our previous work [34]. However, in C-FeOCl, the percentage of Fe(II) and Fe(III) were computed as 27.8% and 72.2%, respectively (Fig. S1b). The results indicate that introducing carbon could reduce a portion of Fe(III) to Fe(II).

Fig. 3d displays the Raman spectra of FeOCl and C-FeOCl. FeOCl exhibits that a band at 322 cm−1 is assigned to the Fe-Cl stretching modes, while those at 390 and 535 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching modes of Fe-O [17]. A peak centered at 720 cm−1 also belongs to FeOCl, consistent with the reported literature [9,35]. In C-FeOCl, the characteristic peaks of FeOCl shifted to the left obviously, which may be caused by embedding carbon dots into the FeOCl layers and introducing carbon atoms into the FeOCl lattice. In addition, four new peaks located at 217, 604, 1289, and 1584 cm−1 appear in C-FeOCl, corresponding to the FeC, FeOC bonds, D (disordered) band, and G (graphitic) bands, respectively [36]. The above characterization results indicate that carbon is successfully introduced into FeOCl in three states, i.e., graphite carbon (on the outer surface of FeOCl), lattice carbon, and carbon dots (between FeOCl layers).

The photo-Fenton performance of all samples was evaluated through TC degradation. As presented in Fig. 4 a, the TC solution (blank) is stable under visible-light irradiation for 15 min. FeOCl and carbon exhibit a low degradation efficiency of 49.2% and 6.9%, respectively. After carbon modification of FeOCl (1C-FeOCl, C-FeOCl, and 2C-FeOCl), TC degradation is remarkably enhanced. Moreover, 60.5% of TC is removed from the Fenton-like system (C-FeOCl/H2O2). The C-FeOCl/H2O2/visible light system has the highest degradation efficiency, reaching 92.4% within 15 min, indicating an effective Fe(II)/Fe(III) cycle. Fig. 4b shows the pseudo-first-oder reaction kinetics of TC degradation. C-FeOCl achieves the highest degradation kinetic constant (k) of 0.1073 min−1 in the photo-Fenton process, which is 2.50, 1.68 and 1.94 times higher than those of FeOCl (0.0428 min−1), 1C-FeOCl (0.0640 min−1) and 2C-FeOCl (0.0552 min−1), respectively. Therefore, in this study, we take C-FeOCl as the subject of discussion. Moreover, C-FeOCl shows good catalytic activity for the degradation of TC in the photo-Fenton process compared to the reported works of literature (Table S1), indicating its promising potential in water decontamination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation mechanism of C-FeOCl based photo-Fenton system
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the mechanism of TC degradation by C-FeOCl in the photo-Fenton process, the optical properties of samples are investigated through UV–vis DRS. As shown in Fig. 5 a, compared to FeOCl, C-FeOCl shows enhanced light absorption in the whole spectral region of 200–800 nm, which is attributed to the introduction of carbon, including graphite carbon, lattice carbon and carbon dots. The bandgap of FeOCl is calculated to be 1.59 eV according to the Tauc plot (Fig. S1b). Fig. 5b displays the Mott-Schottky curve of FeOCl, which indicates that FeOCl is an n-type semiconductor, and the flat-band (Efb) potential is calculated to be 0.83 V vs. NHE. The corresponding conduction band (CB) is obtained to be 0.73 V by Efb-0.1. Furthermore, the valance band (VB) of FeOCl is calculated to be 2.32 V according to
E
VB
=
E CB
+
E g
formula. The result indicates that FeOCl could be excited by visible light.

The generation, separation, and migration of photogenerated carriers (e− and h+) are examined using the photocurrent response, PL, and EIS spectra. The photocurrent response curves of FeOCl, carbon, C-FeOCl are displayed in Fig. 6 a. C-FeOCl exhibits the highest photocurrent density (1.32 × 10−6 A/cm2), exceeding those of FeOCl (0.11 × 10−6 A/cm2) and carbon (0.01 × 10−6 A/cm2). It indicates that more photogenerated e− and h+ are generated and separated on C-FeOCl [37]. The introduction of carbon in/on FeOCl promotes charge transfer and separation, thus reducing the recombination of e− and h+. In other words, the uniformly dense graphite carbon on the external surface of FeOCl accelerates the transportation and separation of photoexcited e− along the horizontal direction of FeOCl. The FeOC bond between the layers eliminates the FeOCl anisotropy on C-FeOCl. Thus, the photogenerated e− is migrated and separated in the vertical direction of FeOCl. Fig. S1c reveals that the TC removal efficiency of MC-FeOCl is lower than that of C-FeOCl, indicating that the effective charge transfer in the horizontal and vertical directions enhances the Fe(II)/Fe(III) cycle. Besides, carbon doped into the FeOCl lattice creates OVs, contributing to the separation of e− and h+. As observed in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence signal of C-FeOCl is much lower than that of FeOCl under light excitation at 340 nm, further confirming the effective separation of e− and h+ [38]. In addition, the peak position of C-FeOCl don’t shift compared to FeOCl, revealing that the introduction of carbon doesn’t affect the energy band structure of FeOCl [39]. EIS spectra (Fig. 6c) show that C-FeOCl has the smallest arc radius, suggesting the most negligible charge transfer resistance in it. As shown in Fig. 6d, C-FeOCl (6.02 ns) has a higher lifetime of charge carriers (τav) than FeOCl (3.79 ns), confirming that carbons in different existence states on FeOCl significantly promote the transfer and separation of charge, thus improving the photo-Fenton activity.

The role of photogenerated e− in C-FeOCl is investigated by in-situ XPS. As shown in Fig. 7 a, XPS spectra of Fe 2p in FeOCl don’t change before and after visible light irradiation. However, Fe 2p in C-FeOCl displays a shift after visible light illumination (Fig. 7b), suggesting the generation of Fe(II) species [40]. Furthermore, in C-FeOCl, Fe 2p peak fitting in Fe(II) increases by 8.7% after visible light irradiation (Fig. S1d), suggesting that the coordinatively unsaturated Fe(III) on the inner and outer surfaces of C-FeOCl capture e− for reduction to Fe(II). The generated Fe(II) species can coordinate with more H2O2 to produce OH.

As shown in Fig. 7c, the EPR spectrum of C-FeOCl has a significant paramagnetic signal with the g value of 2.00 in comparison with FeOCl, suggesting the generation of OVs. This is attributed to carbon atoms replacing a part of Cl atoms on C-FeOCl, which results in many asymmetrically coordinated of Fe-O clusters. Fig. 7d displays the O 1s XPS of FeOCl and C-FeOCl. For FeOCl, the peaks at 531.3 and 532.0 eV are assigned to Fe-O and surface OH, respectively [41]. Three peaks at 530.2, 531.6 and 532.5 eV are identified in C-FeOCl, which are assigned to the Fe-O, CO, and CO, respectively [42]. In addition, O 1s XPS of C-FeOCl has a reduced area of Fe-O than that of FeOCl, further confirming the existence of OVs [43]. On the one hand, the appearance of OVs could produce a lot of CUISs on C-FeOCl, including the outer and inner surfaces of C-FeOCl [44]. It is conducive to coordinating with more H2O2. On the other hand, the OVs can trap electrons and facilitate photogenerated e− and h+ separation [45]. Besides, carbon atoms doped in FeOCl lattice can enhance light adsorption by affecting the electronic environments of Fe cations and O anions [13].

To further explore the mechanism of the photo-Fenton reaction, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) were determined by radical scavenger experiments and EPR measurements. Fig. 8 a shows that OH, O2 −, and h+ involve TC degradation, in which the OH plays a significant role. The production of O2 − may be attributed to the reaction of Fe(III) and H2O2 [46]. In addition, considering the literature that suggests the existence of 1O2 in the photo-Fenton system [47,48], we added l-histidine to C-FeOCl/H2O2/visible light system. The result shows that the removal rate of TC doesn’t decrease, indicating that 1O2 isn’t produced. Furthermore, the generated OH and O2 − are detected by EPR signals of DMPO-OH (Fig. 8b) [49] and DMPO-O2 − (Fig. S4a) under visible light irradiation [46,50], which is consistent with the results of radical scavenger experiments. A stronger signal in the EPR spectra indicates more ROS production. Thus, the amount of OH in C-FeOCl/H2O2/visible light is much higher than that of FeOCl/H2O2/visible light, consistent with the results of TC degradation. The above results suggest that a large amount of Fe(II) on C-FeOCl reacts with H2O2 to generate OH for the degradation of TC under visible light irradiation, demonstrating the existence of a well-established Fe(II)/Fe(III) cycle on C-FeOCl.

DFT calculations revealed the activation process of H2O2 in C-FeOCl. As shown in Fig. S2, the absorption energies of H2O2 at internal and external surfaces (Fe sites) of C-FeOCl are −0.18 (Fig. S2a, c) and −0.84 eV (Fig. S2b, d), respectively, indicating that the incorporation of carbon into FeOCl lattice could enhance the adsorption ability of H2O2. The OO bond lengths in the adsorbed H2O2 at internal and external surfaces (Fe sites) of C-FeOCl are stretched to 2.23 and 2.32 eV, respectively. The above results indicate that the Fe sites on the inner surface of C-FeOCl are more favorable for H2O2 adsorption than those on the outer surface.

Furthermore, H2O2 activation process on internal and external surfaces (CUISs) of C-FeOCl were investigated. Firstly, the H2O2 molecule adsorbs on the Fe sites and is subsequently easily cleaved into two OH groups, one OH can be desorbed into an OH radical, and the other converted into an OH ion (OH−), which can be coupled with the H+ to form H2O. As shown in Fig. 10, C-FeOCl, H2O2 can be readily cleaved into two OH groups with potential barriers of 0.32 (Fig. 9
b) and 0.33 eV (Fig. 9c) on the inner and outer surfaces of C-FeOCl, respectively. These are much lower than FeOCl (0.45 eV) (Fig. 9a). The above results show that the CUISs on the inner and outer surfaces of C-FeOCl can activate H2O2 to OH, which is consistent with the experimental results.

Therefore, based on the above characterization and experimental results, a possible photo-Fenton reaction mechanism is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 10. Firstly, H2O2 affiliates with CUISs located at the internal and external surfaces of C-FeOCl. Following exposure to visible light, FeOCl is excited, and the photogenerated e− and h+ are in the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), respectively. Graphite carbon provides a high specific surface area and electrical conductivity, and the photogenerated e− transfers from graphite carbon to FeOCl. In the horizontal direction of FeOCl, the accumulated photogenerated e− transferred to Fe(III), producing Fe(II). At the same time, the interlayered carbon dots provide a FeOC bridge, allowing e− in the FeOCl plane (including those from the CB with FeOCl and graphitic carbon) to migrate in the vertical direction into the inner surface of FeOCl. The Fe(III) on the inner surface capture e− to produce Fe(II). The Fenton reaction is initiated between Fe(II) (on the external and internal surfaces of FeOCl) and the coordinated H2O2, generating a large number of OH, capable of degrading the TC molecules. After that, Fe(II) is converted to Fe(III). Analogously, the Fe(II)/Fe(III) cycle continues with the assistance of visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of key parameters on TC degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The influences of operating parameters and inorganic ions on photo-Fenton TC degradation by C-FeOCl were investigated considering the complexity of the actual water. As illustrated in Fig. 11 a and Fig. S3a, when the concentration of H2O2 increases from 1 to 3 mL/L, k arises from 0.0635 to 0.1074 min−1. Although the H2O2 concentration increases to 4 mL/L, the k remains practically unchanged (0.1140 min−1), which is attributed that too much H2O2 would trap OH, hindering the degradation of TC [51]. Therefore, 3 mL/L is selected as the optimal H2O2 concentration.

Fig. 11b and Fig. S3b show that the removal rate of TC and k exhibit upward trends (63%-84%, 0.0570–0.1070 min−1, respectively) as the catalyst dose increases from 0.05 to 0.1 g/L. The main reason for this phenomenon is ascribed to the fact that more catalysts could provide more CUISs for ROS production. However, as the catalyst dosage increases to 0.15 g/L, the k reduces from 0.1070 to 0.0950 min−1, assigning that the high catalyst concentration hinders light penetration and thus affects the degradation of TC [52]. The results reveal that the suitable concentration of catalyst is 0.1 g/L.

Fig. 11c and Fig. S3c show high TC degradation efficiencies in the pH range 3.0–9.1, which is superior to conventional Fenton reaction in the treatment of pollutants in water. Notably, the highest removal efficiency of TC is achieved at a neutral pH. It can be explained that the leaching of iron ions become serious under strong acidic conditions (pH < 5), so the degradation of TC is undesirable. At alkaline conditions (pH > 7), H2O2 molecules are unstable, and the generation of OH is inhibited, resulting in the inefficient removal of TC [53].

Four typical inorganic anions (e.g., Cl−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, and HCO3 −) are chosen to study the effects on TC degradation. Fig. 11d and Fig. S3d display that Cl− and SO4 2− have no impact on TC degradation. However, the degradation efficiency of TC decreases to 54% and 55% with the presence of 10 mmol/L CO3 2− and HCO3 −, respectively. On the one hand, CO3 2− and HCO3 − can preferentially consume OH to produce low reactive CO3 − [54,55]; on the other hand, their addition affects the pH of TC solution [56], which both inhibits TC degradation.

The reusability and stability of catalysts are essential for future practical applications. After four cycles, the degradation efficiency of TC could reach up to 74.3% (Fig. 12 a). Mineralization rates of TC slightly decreased (Fig. S4c), implying C-FeOCl possesses desirable reusability. FT-IR (Fig. 12b) and XRD (Fig. S4b) spectra of used C-FeOCl match with those of a fresh sample, further demonstrating the stability of its crystal and surface structure. Besides, the leaching concentration of Fe ions from C-FeOCl meets the discharge standards imposed by European Union (Fig. S4c). It is much lower than that of FeOCl, indicating that introducing carbon results in a more stable structure of FeOCl.

The possible degradation products and pathways of TC are inferred from the HPLC-MS/MS results. The peak in the chromatogram of the first solution with a retention time of 1.86 mins corresponds to TC. The m/z of TC is known to be 445 (Fig. S5a1) [57]. Peak intensity with a retention period of 1.93 mins decreases compared to initial peak intensity, indicating TC degradation (Fig. S5b, c). The peak of a retention duration of 1.93 mins vanishes, as seen in Fig. S5d, revealing that most of the TC have been degraded. Three degradation pathways of TC are proposed as illustrated in Fig. 13 . In pathway I, because of the low bond energy of NC, the methyl group in the molecule of TC is attacked by OH, resulting in the intermediate TC1 (m/z = 417) [27]. The formed TC1 could be dehydrated, and radicals attack the acylamino to produce the intermediate product TC2 (m/z = 362). Next, the D ring in the TC2 is damaged to generate TC3 (m/z = 318). The TC3 is transformed to TC4 (m/z = 274) by losing the carboxyl groups [58]. Regarding Pathway II, TC molecules transform into a product named TC5 (m/z = 461) by breaking a double bond in the C ring of the TC structure while being attacked by OH [59]. OH then targets the double bond in the D ring of TC5 to TC6 (m/z = 477) [60]. Pathway III, the TC is changed into TC7 (m/z = 427) through a dehydration reaction [61]. By losing its amino group and methyl group, the generated TC7 undergoes additional oxidation to yield TC8 (m/z = 369). The alternative degradation pathway is oxidized to generate TC9 (m/z = 396) [62]. These intermediates above are continuously attacked by OH to generate small molecular organics such as TC10 (m/z = 217), TC11(m/z = 152) and TC12 (m/z = 118), which eventually mineralize into CO2, H2O and inorganic ions.

The toxicity of TC and degradation intermediates is evaluated by T.E.S.T software, and the detailed results are presented in Table S2. As displayed in Fig. 14 a, daphnia magna LC50-48 h value of TC is 4.71 mol/L, considered as “toxic”. The degradation intermediates TC1, TC3, TC4, TC7, TC8, and TC9 have lower acute toxicity than TC. However, the degradation products TC2, TC5, TC6, TC10, TC11, and TC12 are more toxic than TC. Similarly, the fathead minnow LC50-96 h values of the intermediates (except for TC2, TC5, TC6, TC10, TC11, and TC12) increase, suggesting the toxicity is reduced after the degradation of TC (Fig. 14b). Fig. 14c exhibits that the developmental toxicity of the intermediates (except TC1, TC3, TC4, TC7, and TC8) significantly decreases. As shown in Fig. 14d, most intermediates show lower mutagenicity toxicity. The TC2 and TC12 are transformed into “mutagenicity negative.” In general, the toxicity of TC is reduced after degradation.

In conclusion, we successfully introduce carbon in three states into FeOCl and demonstrate its excellent Fenton-like performance for TC degradation under visible light. We reveal the different roles of the three types of carbon during photo-Fenton catalysis. Graphite carbon on the outer surface of FeOCl facilitates the migration and separation of photogenerated e− in the horizontal direction of FeOCl. The FeOC bridges provided by interlayered carbon dots accelerate the charge migration-separation in the vertical direction of FeOCl, guaranteeing the effective circulation of Fe(II)/Fe(III). The introduced lattice carbon could expose more CUISs on the internal and external surfaces of FeOCl to coordinate with more H2O2 molecules. DFT calculations indicate a significant decrease in the reaction energy barrier of CUISs in C-FeOCl to H2O2. The catalyst maintains high catalytic activity in the presence of common anions with excellent stability and adaptability. This study offers a new strategy for the photo-Fenton process for water decontamination.
